benchmark: Analyze the development of early civilizations in Mesopotamia, Egypt, the Indus Valley, and China, including their political, economic, and social structures
item_specs: Students analyze the characteristics and achievements of early river valley civilizations
essential_questions: What common features did early civilizations share?
How did geography influence the development of early civilizations?
learning_targets: I can analyze the development of early civilizations
I can compare the political and social structures of ancient societies
sample_assessment:
Which characteristic was shared by ALL early river valley civilizations?
A) Democratic government
B) Development of writing systems and organized agriculture
C) Use of iron tools
D) Monotheistic religion

benchmark: Explain the significance of major world religions and philosophical traditions, including Hinduism, Buddhism, Judaism, Christianity, Islam, and Confucianism
item_specs: Students explain the origins, key beliefs, and influence of major world religions
essential_questions: What are the core beliefs of major world religions?
How have religions shaped civilizations and cultures?
learning_targets: I can explain the key beliefs of major world religions
I can describe how religious and philosophical traditions influenced societies
sample_assessment:
Which religion teaches the concept of the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path as a way to end suffering?
A) Hinduism
B) Buddhism
C) Confucianism
D) Islam

benchmark: Analyze the political and cultural achievements of classical Greece, including democracy, philosophy, and the arts
item_specs: Students analyze how Greek political and cultural achievements influenced Western civilization
essential_questions: How did Greek democracy differ from modern democracy?
What lasting contributions did Greek culture make to Western civilization?
learning_targets: I can analyze the development of Athenian democracy
I can explain the contributions of Greek philosophers and artists
sample_assessment:
Athenian democracy differed from modern American democracy in that Athens practiced:
A) Representative democracy where elected officials governed
B) Direct democracy where citizens voted on laws themselves
C) Constitutional monarchy with elected advisors
D) Oligarchy where wealthy merchants ruled

benchmark: Analyze the political, legal, and engineering achievements of the Roman Republic and Empire, including the development of representative government and Roman law
item_specs: Students analyze how Roman political and legal systems influenced later governments
essential_questions: How did the Roman Republic influence modern government?
What caused the fall of the Roman Empire?
learning_targets: I can analyze the political structure of the Roman Republic
I can explain the significance of Roman law and engineering
sample_assessment:
The Roman concept of 'rule of law' influenced later civilizations by establishing that:
A) The emperor's word was absolute law
B) Laws should apply equally to all citizens, not just the will of rulers
C) Religious leaders should make all laws
D) Only senators could be tried in court

benchmark: Analyze the political, economic, and social structures of medieval Europe, including feudalism, the manor system, and the role of the Catholic Church
item_specs: Students analyze how feudalism and the Church shaped medieval European society
essential_questions: How did feudalism organize medieval European society?
What role did the Catholic Church play in medieval life?
learning_targets: I can analyze the structure of feudal society
I can explain the role of the Catholic Church in medieval Europe
sample_assessment:
In the feudal system, vassals owed their lords:
A) Monetary taxes only
B) Military service and loyalty in exchange for land
C) Religious obedience
D) Merchant goods from trade

benchmark: Analyze the causes, events, and effects of the Crusades on European and Middle Eastern societies
item_specs: Students analyze the motivations for and consequences of the Crusades
essential_questions: What motivated the Crusades?
How did the Crusades affect trade and cultural exchange?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of the Crusades
I can explain how the Crusades influenced trade and cultural exchange
sample_assessment:
A major long-term effect of the Crusades on Europe was:
A) The permanent Christian control of Jerusalem
B) Increased trade with the East and exposure to new goods and ideas
C) The collapse of the feudal system
D) The end of the Catholic Church's influence

benchmark: Analyze the achievements and influence of Islamic civilization during its Golden Age, including advances in science, mathematics, medicine, and architecture
item_specs: Students analyze the intellectual and cultural achievements of the Islamic Golden Age
essential_questions: What achievements defined the Islamic Golden Age?
How did Islamic scholars preserve and advance knowledge?
learning_targets: I can analyze the achievements of Islamic civilization during the Golden Age
I can explain how Islamic scholars preserved classical knowledge
sample_assessment:
During the Islamic Golden Age, scholars made significant contributions to all of the following EXCEPT:
A) Algebra and mathematics
B) Medicine and pharmacology
C) Steam engine technology
D) Astronomy and optics

benchmark: Analyze the development and achievements of civilizations in sub-Saharan Africa, including the kingdoms of Ghana, Mali, and Songhai
item_specs: Students analyze the political and economic structures of West African empires
essential_questions: How did trade shape the development of West African empires?
What were the major achievements of African kingdoms?
learning_targets: I can analyze the development of West African kingdoms
I can explain the role of trans-Saharan trade in African civilizations
sample_assessment:
Mansa Musa's famous pilgrimage to Mecca in 1324 demonstrated the wealth of the Mali Empire by:
A) Conquering every kingdom along the route
B) Distributing so much gold that he temporarily lowered its value in the regions he visited
C) Building the Great Mosque of Timbuktu before his journey
D) Establishing diplomatic relations with China

benchmark: Analyze the political, social, and economic systems of major East Asian civilizations, including China and Japan
item_specs: Students analyze the achievements and structures of Chinese and Japanese civilizations
essential_questions: How did Confucianism shape Chinese government and society?
What characterized Japanese feudal society?
learning_targets: I can analyze the political and social systems of Chinese dynasties
I can compare Chinese and Japanese feudal societies
sample_assessment:
The Chinese civil service examination system was significant because it:
A) Limited government jobs to the nobility
B) Selected government officials based on merit and knowledge rather than birth
C) Was used only during the Han Dynasty
D) Focused exclusively on military skills

benchmark: Analyze the development of the Mongol Empire and its impact on trade, cultural exchange, and political structures across Eurasia
item_specs: Students analyze how the Mongol Empire facilitated trade and cultural exchange
essential_questions: How did the Mongol Empire connect different parts of Eurasia?
What were the positive and negative effects of Mongol conquest?
learning_targets: I can analyze the expansion and impact of the Mongol Empire
I can explain how Pax Mongolica facilitated trade along the Silk Road
sample_assessment:
The Pax Mongolica benefited Eurasian trade by:
A) Destroying all trade routes between East and West
B) Providing safe passage along trade routes and encouraging cultural exchange
C) Converting all conquered peoples to a single religion
D) Establishing a single currency across the empire

benchmark: Analyze the political, economic, and social developments of Mesoamerican and South American civilizations, including the Maya, Aztec, and Inca
item_specs: Students analyze the achievements and structures of pre-Columbian civilizations
essential_questions: What achievements defined the Maya, Aztec, and Inca civilizations?
How did geography influence these civilizations?
learning_targets: I can analyze the achievements of Mesoamerican and South American civilizations
I can compare the political and social structures of the Maya, Aztec, and Inca
sample_assessment:
The Inca Empire's system of quipu was used for:
A) Religious ceremonies and sacrifices
B) Recording numerical data and keeping records using knotted strings
C) Irrigating farmland in the Andes
D) Building roads across mountains

benchmark: Analyze the causes and effects of the Renaissance, including the revival of classical learning, humanism, and artistic achievements
item_specs: Students analyze how the Renaissance transformed European thought, art, and society
essential_questions: What caused the Renaissance?
How did humanism change European thought and culture?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and characteristics of the Renaissance
I can explain how Renaissance ideas spread across Europe
sample_assessment:
Renaissance humanism differed from medieval thinking primarily by emphasizing:
A) Religious devotion above all other pursuits
B) Individual achievement, classical learning, and human potential
C) Military conquest and territorial expansion
D) Rejection of all religious beliefs

benchmark: Analyze the causes and effects of the Protestant Reformation and the Catholic Counter-Reformation
item_specs: Students analyze how the Reformation transformed European religion and politics
essential_questions: What caused the Protestant Reformation?
How did the Reformation change European politics and society?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of the Protestant Reformation
I can explain the Catholic Church's response to the Reformation
sample_assessment:
Martin Luther's 95 Theses primarily challenged the Catholic Church's practice of:
A) Requiring Sunday attendance at mass
B) Selling indulgences to forgive sins
C) Using Latin in church services
D) Appointing bishops

benchmark: Analyze the causes and effects of European exploration and colonization, including the Columbian Exchange and the Atlantic slave trade
item_specs: Students analyze the motivations for and consequences of European exploration and colonization
essential_questions: What motivated European exploration?
How did the Columbian Exchange transform the world?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of European exploration
I can explain the impact of the Columbian Exchange on global populations
sample_assessment:
The Columbian Exchange refers to the:
A) Trade of gold between Europe and Africa
B) Transfer of plants, animals, diseases, and ideas between the Eastern and Western hemispheres
C) Exchange of prisoners during colonial wars
D) Trading of enslaved people between Africa and the Americas only

benchmark: Analyze the development of the Ottoman, Safavid, and Mughal empires and their contributions to art, architecture, and culture
item_specs: Students analyze the political structures and cultural achievements of Islamic empires
essential_questions: What characterized the great Islamic empires of the early modern period?
How did these empires contribute to world culture?
learning_targets: I can analyze the political and cultural achievements of the Ottoman, Safavid, and Mughal empires
I can compare the governance structures of these empires
sample_assessment:
The Ottoman Empire under Suleiman the Magnificent was known for:
A) Its isolation from European affairs
B) A sophisticated legal code, military power, and religious tolerance for non-Muslims
C) Its reliance on nomadic herding
D) Banning all forms of artistic expression

benchmark: Analyze the causes and effects of the Scientific Revolution, including the contributions of Copernicus, Galileo, Newton, and the development of the scientific method
item_specs: Students analyze how the Scientific Revolution changed the way people understood the natural world
essential_questions: How did the Scientific Revolution change the way people understood the world?
Why was the scientific method a revolutionary development?
learning_targets: I can analyze the contributions of key Scientific Revolution figures
I can explain the significance of the scientific method
sample_assessment:
The heliocentric model proposed by Copernicus challenged the existing worldview by arguing that:
A) The Earth was flat
B) The Sun, not the Earth, was at the center of the solar system
C) The universe was infinite
D) Gravity controlled planetary motion

benchmark: Analyze the major ideas of the Enlightenment and their influence on political revolutions, including the concepts of natural rights, social contract, and separation of powers
item_specs: Students analyze how Enlightenment ideas challenged traditional authority and inspired revolutions
essential_questions: How did Enlightenment ideas challenge traditional authority?
How did Enlightenment philosophy influence political revolutions?
learning_targets: I can analyze the major ideas of Enlightenment thinkers
I can explain how Enlightenment ideas influenced political revolutions
sample_assessment:
Montesquieu's concept of separation of powers directly influenced the structure of:
A) The French monarchy under Louis XVI
B) The United States Constitution's three branches of government
C) The British parliamentary system
D) The Russian czarist government

benchmark: Analyze the causes, events, and effects of the French Revolution and the rise and fall of Napoleon Bonaparte
item_specs: Students analyze how the French Revolution transformed European politics and society
essential_questions: What caused the French Revolution?
How did Napoleon's rise and fall reshape Europe?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of the French Revolution
I can explain how Napoleon's conquests changed Europe
sample_assessment:
The Congress of Vienna (1814-1815) aimed to:
A) Spread democratic ideals across Europe
B) Restore a balance of power and return Europe to pre-Napoleonic stability
C) Establish Napoleon as emperor of a united Europe
D) Create an economic union among European nations

benchmark: Analyze the causes, events, and effects of Latin American independence movements, including the contributions of Simon Bolivar and Miguel Hidalgo
item_specs: Students analyze how Enlightenment ideas and colonial grievances fueled Latin American independence
essential_questions: What factors led to Latin American independence movements?
How did leaders like Bolivar and Hidalgo shape independence?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes of Latin American independence movements
I can explain the contributions of key independence leaders
sample_assessment:
Simon Bolivar is often called 'The Liberator' because he:
A) Freed enslaved people throughout the Caribbean
B) Led independence movements that freed several South American countries from Spanish rule
C) Wrote the Declaration of the Rights of Man
D) Established democratic governments across Central America

benchmark: Analyze the causes and effects of the Industrial Revolution, including technological innovations, urbanization, and changes in labor practices
item_specs: Students analyze how industrialization transformed economies, societies, and daily life
essential_questions: How did the Industrial Revolution transform society?
What were the social costs and benefits of industrialization?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of the Industrial Revolution
I can explain how industrialization changed labor practices and daily life
sample_assessment:
A major social effect of the Industrial Revolution was:
A) The decline of cities and growth of rural populations
B) The growth of a middle class and the expansion of factory labor in urban areas
C) The elimination of poverty across Europe
D) The end of international trade

benchmark: Analyze the political and social responses to industrialization, including socialism, communism, and the labor movement
item_specs: Students analyze how political ideologies emerged in response to industrial capitalism
essential_questions: How did people respond to the inequalities created by industrialization?
How did Karl Marx's ideas challenge capitalism?
learning_targets: I can analyze the political responses to industrialization
I can compare socialism, communism, and capitalism
sample_assessment:
Karl Marx argued that history was driven by:
A) Religious conflict between faiths
B) Class struggle between the bourgeoisie (owners) and the proletariat (workers)
C) Competition between nations for territory
D) Technological innovation and scientific discovery

benchmark: Analyze the causes, course, and consequences of European imperialism in Africa, Asia, and the Pacific
item_specs: Students analyze the motivations for and effects of European imperialism
essential_questions: What motivated European imperialism?
How did imperialism affect colonized peoples?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of European imperialism
I can explain how imperialism affected colonized societies
sample_assessment:
The Berlin Conference of 1884-1885 was significant because it:
A) Ended World War I
B) Divided Africa among European powers with little regard for existing African boundaries
C) Established trade agreements between Europe and China
D) Granted independence to all African colonies

benchmark: Analyze the causes, major events, and consequences of World War I, including the alliance system, trench warfare, and the Treaty of Versailles
item_specs: Students analyze the causes, conduct, and aftermath of WWI
essential_questions: What caused World War I?
How did the Treaty of Versailles set the stage for future conflicts?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and consequences of World War I
I can explain how the Treaty of Versailles affected post-war Europe
sample_assessment:
The Treaty of Versailles contributed to future instability in Europe by:
A) Creating a strong League of Nations
B) Imposing harsh reparations and territorial losses on Germany, fueling resentment
C) Granting self-determination to all European peoples
D) Establishing a lasting peace between France and Germany

benchmark: Analyze the causes and effects of the Russian Revolution, including the rise of communism under Lenin and Stalin
item_specs: Students analyze how the Russian Revolution led to the establishment of the Soviet Union
essential_questions: What caused the Russian Revolution?
How did communist rule change Russian society?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of the Russian Revolution
I can explain how Stalin's policies transformed the Soviet Union
sample_assessment:
Stalin's policy of collectivization aimed to:
A) Distribute land equally to all peasants
B) Consolidate small farms into large state-run agricultural operations
C) Encourage private farming and free markets
D) Industrialize cities by moving peasants to factories

benchmark: Analyze the rise of totalitarian regimes in the 20th century, including fascism in Italy and Germany, and militarism in Japan
item_specs: Students analyze how totalitarian leaders rose to power and controlled societies
essential_questions: What conditions allowed totalitarian regimes to rise?
How did totalitarian leaders maintain power?
learning_targets: I can analyze the rise of totalitarian regimes
I can compare fascist, communist, and militarist governments
sample_assessment:
The policy of appeasement, exemplified by the Munich Agreement of 1938, failed because:
A) It strengthened the League of Nations
B) It encouraged Hitler's further aggression by rewarding territorial demands
C) It led to an immediate declaration of war
D) It prevented Germany from rearming

benchmark: Analyze the causes, major events, and global consequences of World War II, including the Holocaust, the use of atomic weapons, and the establishment of the United Nations
item_specs: Students analyze WWII's major events and lasting global consequences
essential_questions: What were the major turning points of World War II?
How did WWII reshape the global order?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and consequences of World War II
I can explain the significance of the Holocaust and atomic weapons
sample_assessment:
The Nuremberg Trials were significant because they:
A) Ended World War II
B) Established the principle that individuals could be held accountable for crimes against humanity
C) Created the United Nations
D) Divided Germany into East and West

benchmark: Analyze the origins, major events, and effects of the Cold War, including the division of Europe, nuclear proliferation, and proxy wars
item_specs: Students analyze Cold War tensions and their global effects
essential_questions: What caused the Cold War?
How did the Cold War affect nations around the world?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of the Cold War
I can explain how proxy wars extended Cold War tensions globally
sample_assessment:
The Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962 was the closest the Cold War came to nuclear war because:
A) Cuba invaded the United States
B) The Soviet Union placed nuclear missiles in Cuba, within range of the U.S.
C) The U.S. tested nuclear weapons near Cuba
D) NATO forces surrounded the Soviet Union

benchmark: Analyze the process and challenges of decolonization in Africa and Asia, including the independence movements in India, Vietnam, and South Africa
item_specs: Students analyze how colonized nations gained independence and the challenges they faced
essential_questions: What strategies did colonized peoples use to achieve independence?
What challenges did newly independent nations face?
learning_targets: I can analyze the process of decolonization in Africa and Asia
I can explain the challenges faced by newly independent nations
sample_assessment:
Mohandas Gandhi's strategy of nonviolent resistance was effective in India's independence movement because:
A) It led to immediate British withdrawal
B) It gained widespread international sympathy and moral authority against British colonial rule
C) It was supported by the British government
D) It involved no economic disruption to British interests

benchmark: Analyze the causes and effects of the collapse of the Soviet Union and the end of the Cold War
item_specs: Students analyze the factors that led to the end of the Cold War and Soviet dissolution
essential_questions: What caused the collapse of the Soviet Union?
How did the end of the Cold War change the global order?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes of the Soviet Union's collapse
I can explain how the end of the Cold War reshaped global politics
sample_assessment:
Gorbachev's policies of glasnost (openness) and perestroika (restructuring) contributed to the collapse of the Soviet Union by:
A) Strengthening communist control over Eastern Europe
B) Exposing economic weaknesses and encouraging demands for political freedom
C) Increasing military spending to unsustainable levels
D) Ending all trade with Western nations

benchmark: Analyze the causes and effects of genocide and human rights violations in the 20th century, including the Armenian Genocide, the Holocaust, and the Rwandan Genocide
item_specs: Students analyze the causes of genocide and international responses to human rights violations
essential_questions: What conditions allow genocide to occur?
How has the international community responded to genocide?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of 20th-century genocides
I can explain the development of international human rights protections
sample_assessment:
The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948) was adopted by the United Nations primarily in response to:
A) The Cold War
B) The atrocities of World War II, including the Holocaust
C) The decolonization of Africa
D) The Russian Revolution

benchmark: Analyze the causes and effects of globalization, including economic interdependence, cultural diffusion, technological advances, and environmental challenges
item_specs: Students analyze how globalization has transformed economies, cultures, and international relations
essential_questions: What are the causes and effects of globalization?
How has globalization created both opportunities and challenges?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of globalization
I can evaluate the benefits and challenges of economic interdependence
sample_assessment:
A major criticism of economic globalization is that it:
A) Has completely eliminated poverty worldwide
B) Can lead to exploitation of workers in developing countries and widening income inequality
C) Has stopped international trade
D) Has eliminated cultural differences between nations

benchmark: Analyze the rise of terrorism as a global issue, including the causes, major events, and international responses to terrorism in the 21st century
item_specs: Students analyze the causes of terrorism and evaluate international responses
essential_questions: What factors contribute to the rise of terrorism?
How have nations responded to the threat of terrorism?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes and effects of global terrorism
I can evaluate international responses to terrorism
sample_assessment:
International cooperation against terrorism is challenging primarily because:
A) All nations agree on the definition of terrorism
B) Terrorist organizations operate across national borders, making unilateral action insufficient
C) Terrorism only affects developing nations
D) The United Nations has no role in counterterrorism

benchmark: Analyze the impact of technological innovation on global communication, commerce, and social movements in the 21st century
item_specs: Students analyze how technology has transformed global interaction and activism
essential_questions: How has technology transformed global communication and commerce?
What role has social media played in social and political movements?
learning_targets: I can analyze the impact of technology on global communication
I can explain how social media has influenced political and social movements
sample_assessment:
Social media played a significant role in the Arab Spring uprisings by:
A) Replacing traditional governments with digital democracies
B) Allowing protestors to organize, share information, and gain international attention rapidly
C) Providing military support to rebels
D) Preventing government censorship entirely

benchmark: Use geographic tools and data to analyze patterns of human settlement, migration, and resource distribution throughout world history
item_specs: Students use geographic analysis to understand historical patterns and developments
essential_questions: How has geography influenced human migration and settlement?
How do patterns of resource distribution affect global power?
learning_targets: I can use geographic tools to analyze historical patterns
I can explain how geography influenced migration and resource distribution
sample_assessment:
Throughout history, human migration has been most influenced by:
A) Artistic and cultural preferences
B) Access to resources, economic opportunity, and escape from conflict or persecution
C) Climate change exclusively
D) Government relocation programs

benchmark: Analyze the development of economic systems throughout world history, including mercantilism, capitalism, socialism, and mixed economies
item_specs: Students compare economic systems and analyze their development over time
essential_questions: How have economic systems evolved throughout history?
What are the strengths and weaknesses of different economic systems?
learning_targets: I can analyze the development of major economic systems
I can compare the features of capitalism, socialism, and mixed economies
sample_assessment:
A key difference between capitalism and socialism is that capitalism emphasizes:
A) Government ownership of all businesses
B) Private ownership of property and free-market competition
C) Equal distribution of wealth by the government
D) Central planning of the economy

benchmark: Evaluate the role of international organizations in maintaining peace, promoting human rights, and addressing global challenges
item_specs: Students evaluate the effectiveness of international organizations in addressing global issues
essential_questions: How effective are international organizations in maintaining peace?
What role do international organizations play in addressing global challenges?
learning_targets: I can evaluate the role of international organizations
I can analyze how global cooperation addresses international challenges
sample_assessment:
A major limitation of the United Nations in maintaining global peace is:
A) It has no member nations
B) Security Council members with veto power can block resolutions, even when action is needed
C) It has unlimited military forces to enforce decisions
D) It only addresses environmental issues

benchmark: Analyze how environmental factors and natural resources have influenced historical events, economic development, and conflicts throughout world history
item_specs: Students analyze the relationship between environmental factors and historical developments
essential_questions: How have environmental factors shaped world history?
How have conflicts over natural resources influenced global politics?
learning_targets: I can analyze how environmental factors influenced historical events
I can explain how resource competition has driven conflict
sample_assessment:
Competition for control of oil resources in the Middle East has been a major factor in:
A) The development of renewable energy in the 19th century
B) Regional and international conflicts and the strategic importance of the Middle East
C) The decline of industrialization in Europe
D) The formation of the European Union

benchmark: Analyze the role of women in different societies throughout world history, including their contributions to political, economic, and social change
item_specs: Students analyze how women's roles have changed across different eras and cultures
essential_questions: How have women's roles changed throughout world history?
What contributions have women made to political and social movements?
learning_targets: I can analyze the changing roles of women throughout world history
I can explain women's contributions to political and social movements
sample_assessment:
The global women's suffrage movement was significant because it:
A) Immediately granted all women worldwide the right to vote
B) Led to the gradual expansion of voting rights for women across many nations during the 19th and 20th centuries
C) Was limited to the United States and Britain
D) Focused only on economic rights, not political participation

benchmark: Use historical thinking skills to analyze cause and effect, compare perspectives, and evaluate the credibility of primary and secondary sources across world history
item_specs: Students apply historical thinking skills to analyze and interpret world history
essential_questions: How do historians analyze cause and effect in world history?
Why is it important to evaluate the credibility of historical sources?
learning_targets: I can analyze cause and effect relationships in world history
I can evaluate the credibility and perspective of historical sources
sample_assessment:
When evaluating a primary source, a historian should consider all of the following EXCEPT:
A) The author's point of view and potential biases
B) The historical context in which it was created
C) Whether the source confirms the historian's personal opinion
D) How the source compares to other evidence from the same period

benchmark: Analyze the causes and effects of the Chinese Communist Revolution and the establishment of the People's Republic of China under Mao Zedong
item_specs: Students analyze how the Communist Revolution transformed China
essential_questions: What caused the Chinese Communist Revolution?
How did Mao's policies transform Chinese society?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes of the Chinese Communist Revolution
I can explain the effects of Mao's policies on Chinese society
sample_assessment:
Mao Zedong's Great Leap Forward (1958-1962) resulted in:
A) Rapid industrial growth and prosperity
B) Widespread famine and millions of deaths due to failed agricultural and industrial policies
C) The establishment of democratic elections in China
D) A successful transition to a market economy

benchmark: Analyze the partition of India in 1947 and its lasting effects on the Indian subcontinent, including Hindu-Muslim tensions and the creation of Pakistan
item_specs: Students analyze the causes and consequences of the partition of India
essential_questions: What led to the partition of India?
What were the human and political consequences of partition?
learning_targets: I can analyze the causes of India's partition
I can explain the lasting effects of partition on South Asia
sample_assessment:
The partition of British India in 1947 created the separate nations of India and Pakistan primarily because of:
A) Economic differences between regions
B) Religious tensions between Hindu and Muslim populations
C) Language differences between north and south
D) Colonial boundaries drawn by the British
